title: JavaScript Closures Knowledge Check
description: Test your understanding of closures, closure scope, shared state, private data, shadowing, and loop callback behavior.
author: DailyCoder

options.text: A function that remembers variables from the scope where it was created
A variable that can only store objects
A loop that stops when a function returns

questions.explanation: A closure is a function bundled with access to the lexical environment where it was created.
The returned inner function closes over `message`, so it can still read it after `outer()` has finished.
Both calls use the same preserved `count` variable, so the value increases from `1` to `2`.
Each call to `createCounter()` creates a separate closure environment. `first` and `second` do not share `count`.

options.text: A different `count` variable for each method

questions.explanation: Both methods are created in the same outer function call, so they close over the same `count` variable.
The inner `message` shadows the outer `message`, but it does not change it.
`inner` does not declare a new `count`, so JavaScript finds and updates the outer `count` variable.
`var` is function-scoped, so all callbacks share the same `i`. After the loop ends, `i` is `4`.
`let` creates a fresh binding for each loop iteration, so each callback closes over a different `i`.

questions.prompt: Why can closures be used for private data?

options.text: Because outer variables become global automatically
Because returned functions can access outer variables while outside code cannot access them directly

questions.explanation: A closure can expose controlled functions while keeping the actual variables hidden inside the outer function scope.

options.text: A function factory using a closure

questions.explanation: `createFormatter` returns a new function that remembers `prefix`, which makes it a function factory using closure.
The returned function can access `name`, `type`, and `theme` through the preserved scope chain.

options.text: Outside code receives the same array and can mutate it

questions.explanation: Returning the array directly exposes the same object reference. Returning a copy, such as `[...items]`, better protects private state.

questions.prompt: Which statement about closures is most accurate?

options.text: Closures only work with global variables
Closures preserve access to variables from the lexical scope where the function was created
Closures copy every variable value once and then freeze it
Closures prevent functions from being returned

questions.explanation: Closures are based on lexical scope. A function keeps access to variables from the environment where it was created.
